nativeRange.habitat: Wetlands, marshes, and roadside ditches across the southeastern United States; amphibious and capable of growing partially or fully submerged

decorPreferences: open background placement with room to grow tall
bright lighting to develop red/burgundy coloration

summary: Ludwigia Peruensis is a red-to-burgundy stem plant sold widely in the aquarium hobby under that name, though botanically it is closely associated with Ludwigia glandulosa, a species native to southeastern US wetlands. It produces its deepest color under high light, CO2, and iron-rich fertilization, with distinctly larger, spade-shaped leaves compared to the similar Ludwigia repens.
careNotes: Ludwigia Peruensis is widely sold in the aquarium trade as a red background stem plant, and most hobbyist sources treat the name as a trade designation for Ludwigia glandulosa — a species native to wetlands and roadside ditches across the southeastern United States, despite the South American-sounding epithet. Coloration is the primary draw: under low or medium light the plant tends to stay olive-green to brown, but given high light (around 30–50 PAR), pressurized CO2 (around 20–30 mg/L), and consistent dosing of iron and micronutrients, the leaves shift toward burgundy, copper-red, and deep red, most noticeably on the upper portion of the stem. A nutrient-rich substrate — aquasoil or root tabs — is strongly recommended, as iron deficiency or chronically insufficient light is the most common reason the plant fails to develop the vivid red color it is sold for. Propagation is straightforward by stem cuttings, and the plant tends to respond to regular topping with bushier lateral growth rather than a single tall stem. Like other stem plants from emersed or tissue-culture stock, freshly planted cuttings may drop their first set of leaves during the transition to fully submerged growth; new submersed foliage generally follows within one to two weeks under good conditions.
breedingNotes: Ludwigia Peruensis is propagated by stem cuttings: a healthy stem top or side shoot of about 5–10 cm with a few leaves is trimmed, the lowest leaf pair is removed, and the cutting is replanted directly into the substrate, where it tends to root quickly. Regularly topping the tallest stems encourages the mother plant to produce bushier lateral side-shoot growth rather than continuing to elongate. Emersed flowering and seed propagation are not commonly practiced in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for trade name (Ludwigia glandulosa peruensis), origin (North America), lighting (medium-high, stronger light intensifies red coloration), CO2 (recommended but not strictly required), growth rate (moderate-to-fast), placement (mid-to-background), temperature (72-82 °F / 22-28 °C), propagation (stem-top and side-shoot cuttings), fertilization (iron-rich), care difficulty (moderate), leaf shape (small spade-shaped), and color range (olive-brown to deep red/burgundy depending on light).

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Onagraceae), native range (southeastern USA), lighting (bright; green-to-red color tradeoff), CO2 (beneficial), growth rate (normal), placement (midground/background), substrate (fine gravel + supplementation), temperature (22-28 °C), pH (6.5-7.4), hardness (4-18 dGH), height (40-55 cm), and care level (medium).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Ludwigia glandulosa
additional.notes: Used as botanical reference for the senior synonym Ludwigia glandulosa: family Onagraceae, order Myrtales, native to wetlands and roadside ditches of the southeastern United States, amphibious habit (grows partially or fully submerged), maximum height ~40 cm, pH ~6-7.5, red/purple coloration with adequate light fading to green under low light. Confirms the taxonomic identity behind the 'peruensis' trade name (the South-American epithet is widely treated as a hobby misnomer).
